range.nativeRange: Central America and Mesoamerica, with domestication centers in both Middle America (Mexico/Guatemala, giving rise to small-seeded types) and the Andes of South America (giving rise to large-seeded types such as kidney, cannellini, and cranberry beans); now one of the most widely cultivated food plants in the world

usNativeStatus.description: Not native to the United States; Phaseolus vulgaris is a New World domesticate originating south of the US border (Mexico through South America). It does not naturalize in the lower 48 — as a strict annual killed by frost, it cannot establish persistent wild populations in temperate North America. Several wild Phaseolus species are native to the southwestern US (notably P. acutifolius and P. metcalfei in Arizona and New Mexico), but P. vulgaris itself is entirely a cultivated crop in the US

invasive.description: No — strict annual; dies completely at frost; seeds do not establish wild populations in temperate or subtropical climates anywhere in the lower 48. Not listed on any state noxious weed list or the federal noxious weed list. No invasive potential in any US region

zone.description: Annual grown in all USDA zones; warm-season crop; not frost tolerant at any stage. Heat zone upper limit is not a practical concern — beans tolerate summer heat across the southern US provided adequate moisture is supplied, though pod set declines above 90°F and flower drop increases above 95°F. In the coldest zones (3-4), the short frost-free season limits dry bean production to early-maturing varieties (90 days or less)

description.height: Bush types: 18-24" compact, self-supporting mounds. Pole types: 6-10' climbing vines requiring a trellis or support structure; will climb any available vertical surface by twining
description.spread: Bush types: 12-18" per plant. Pole types: reach outward 12-18" while climbing vertically
description.spacing: Bush: 4-6" between seeds in rows, rows 18-24" apart; thin to 6" after emergence. Pole: 4-6" between seeds at base of trellis; space trellis structures 18-24" apart. Direct sow; do not transplant — beans resent root disturbance
description.growthRate: Fast — germinates in 7-14 days in warm soil (60°F+); rapid vegetative growth; bush types reach harvestable stage in 45-60 days from sowing; pole types 60-75 days
description.timeToMaturity: Snap/green beans: 45-60 days (bush), 60-75 days (pole) from direct sow to first harvest. Shell beans: harvest when pods are plump and seeds fully formed but not yet dry, typically 70-85 days. Dry beans: leave pods on the plant until fully mature and papery-dry, typically 90-110 days — or complete drying indoors after pulling plants before frost
description.lifespan: Strict annual — one growing season; killed by the first frost; plants naturally senesce after full seed maturation; pole beans can produce continuously for the entire frost-free season if picked regularly; bush beans set all pods at once and are done
description.winterInterest: None — plant is dead by first frost

lightAndWater.sun: Full sun essential — minimum 6-8 hours direct sun; bean plants in shade produce weak, spindly growth with reduced yield and increased disease susceptibility; do not plant where trees, trellises, or structures create afternoon shade
lightAndWater.water: Moderate and consistent — beans need even moisture from germination through pod development; drought stress during flowering causes blossom drop and pod abortion; drought during pod fill causes tough, stringy beans; do not overhead water during flowering (promotes fungal disease and interferes with pollination); drip irrigation or careful soaker hose placement at the base is ideal; avoid waterlogging, which impairs root nodule formation and causes root rot. In the humid East, rainfall often supplies most of the need except during dry spells in July-August; in the arid West and Southwest, irrigation is essential throughout the growing season

drainagePreference.description: Well-drained — beans tolerate average soil moisture but not standing water; waterlogged roots cannot form active Rhizobium nodules and the nitrogen-fixing benefit is lost; saturated soil also promotes Pythium root rot; raised beds provide the drainage advantage this crop rewards. Good internal drainage is more important than surface drainage — a slightly sloped site with heavy subsoil can still waterlog bean roots

claySoilPerformance.description: Moderate — beans can be grown in clay soil but performance is significantly reduced without amendment. Clay causes two compounding problems: (1) waterlogging impairs Rhizobium nodule formation and promotes root rot, and (2) compaction physically restricts the shallow fibrous root system. Cold clay soil also stays wet and cool longer in spring, delaying safe sowing dates by 1-3 weeks compared to lighter soils. Amendment with 3-4" of compost worked into the top 8-10" improves drainage and aeration enough for acceptable performance. Raised beds filled with amended soil are the most reliable approach for heavy clay sites. In-ground clay plantings benefit from permanent raised rows (even 4-6" of mounding improves drainage) and heavy organic mulch

phRange.description: 6.0-7.0; slightly acidic to neutral preferred; Rhizobium nodule formation is impaired below pH 6.0; lime to adjust if needed before sowing

soil.texturePreference: Moderately fertile, well-drained loam is ideal; beans are not heavy feeders and do not require rich soil — excessive nitrogen actually suppresses the nitrogen-fixation that is the plant's ecological and garden function. Clay soils are tolerated if drainage is adequate and soil is not compacted; roots need aeration for nodule activity. Sandy soils drain well but may require more frequent watering. Amend heavy clay with compost (not chemical N fertilizer) before planting
soil.rootCharacter: Fibrous, shallow to moderate root system in the top 12-18" of soil; roots are colonized by Rhizobium leguminosarum bacteria forming pink, active root nodules that fix atmospheric nitrogen — this is one of the most directly useful soil-building processes available to the vegetable gardener; disturbing the roots at harvest eliminates this benefit prematurely, so cut spent plants at the soil surface rather than pulling them, leaving the nitrogen-rich root system to decompose in place

bloom.bloomTime: June-September, depending on sowing date and succession; bush beans bloom in a concentrated flush 40-50 days after sowing; pole beans bloom continuously from about 55 days after sowing through frost if pods are regularly harvested
bloom.bloomDescription: Small, self-fertile pea-family flowers borne in clusters at leaf axils; color varies by type — white or cream (most green bean varieties), pink to lilac (Borlotti, Cranberry), pale purple (Royal Burgundy), scarlet (runner bean — Phaseolus coccineus, a related species); flowers are not showy from a distance but are attractive close up; the flower structure is typical of papilionoid legumes, with a banner petal, two wing petals, and a keel that encloses the stamens
bloom.flowerSex: Hermaphrodite — each flower contains both stamens and pistil; self-pollination occurs within the closed flower before it opens, making beans almost entirely self-fertile
bloom.pollination: Primarily self-pollinating; bees visit for nectar and contribute to pollination but are not required for fruit set; beans do not need multiple plants or cross-pollination; varieties do not cross-pollinate significantly in a home garden (flowers self before bee visit), making seed-saving straightforward

yield.edibleParts:
Phaseolus vulgaris offers three distinct harvest stages, each yielding a different product — understanding these stages is the key to getting maximum value from this plant across the full growing season.
SNAP / GREEN BEANS (immature pod): Harvested when pods are young, tender, and fully elongated but seeds inside are still tiny and undeveloped — typically at 4-6" for standard varieties, 8-10" for French filet types. The entire pod is eaten, raw or cooked. This is the most familiar harvest stage. For maximum flavor and tenderness, harvest daily during peak production — pods left on the plant past their prime rapidly become tough and fibrous, and unpicked pods signal the plant to stop producing (critical for pole beans). Snap beans are also called string beans (older varieties had a fibrous "string" along the seam; modern varieties are stringless) and wax beans (yellow-podded varieties). Purple snap beans (Royal Burgundy) turn green when cooked. Pick with a sharp twist or scissors; never yank, which damages the vine.
SHELL BEANS (mature fresh seeds): Harvested when pods have swollen visibly, the seeds inside are fully formed and plump, but the pods have not yet dried and are still somewhat green to mottled. The pod is discarded; the seeds are shelled out fresh. Shell beans cook faster than dry beans, have a creamier texture, and a richer flavor than either snap beans or fully dried beans. Many varieties grown primarily as dry beans can be harvested at this stage for a superior fresh eating experience — Dragon Tongue (yellow wax, mottled purple), Cannellini, Cranberry/Borlotti, and Flageolet are excellent at this stage. Shell bean season is a distinct window that most gardeners miss by either harvesting too early (snap) or waiting for full dry (dry bean).
DRY BEANS (fully mature dried seeds): Harvested when pods are fully papery, tan, and dry, rattling when shaken; seeds are hard and fully dried. Pull the entire plant, hang it upside down in a dry location to finish curing for 1-2 weeks, then shell out the beans by hand or by thrashing the dry plants in a bag or bucket. Store in airtight jars in a cool, dark location — properly dried beans store for 1-2 years with good germination and flavor, and indefinitely (though cooking time increases). Black Turtle, Pinto, Jacob's Cattle, Kidney, Navy, and Hidatsa Red are grown primarily as dry beans. In short-season areas (Zones 3-5), dry beans need careful variety selection — choose 90-day or shorter varieties and sow as early as soil temperature allows to ensure full maturation before fall frost.
yield.harvestSeason: Bush snap beans: concentrated harvest window, typically 1-2 weeks per sowing; succession planting every 2-3 weeks from last frost through midsummer produces continuous snap bean harvests through early fall. Pole snap beans: continuous harvest from approximately 60-75 days after sowing through first frost — one planting produces all season. Shell beans: a distinct mid-season window (typically August in Zones 5-7). Dry beans: mature late summer to early fall; must be harvested before first frost damages pods (the seeds themselves tolerate some cold, but frost-damaged pods rot before drying)
yield.medicinalUses: Beans are among the most nutritionally complete plant foods available — high protein (20-25% dry weight), soluble fiber (lowers LDL cholesterol), complex carbohydrates with low glycemic index, significant iron, magnesium, potassium, zinc, and B vitamins (especially folate). The soluble fiber feeds beneficial gut bacteria (prebiotic effect). Traditional medicine attributes kidney-supportive and blood-sugar-stabilizing properties to bean consumption. Not a first-aid or acute medicinal plant, but one of the most valuable long-term health foods available from a kitchen garden
yield.craftOtherYield: Dried pods of some varieties (particularly speckled and colored dry beans) are decorative. Dried bean vines make adequate compostable garden twine in a pinch. The cut root system left in the soil after harvest is a genuine nitrogen deposit for the following crop — quantified at 40-150 lbs of nitrogen per acre in agricultural settings; meaningful at garden scale after successive years of bean growing

cautionsAndHazards.toxicityPeople: Raw beans (particularly kidney beans) contain high concentrations of phytohaemagglutinin (PHA, a lectin) that causes severe nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea if eaten undercooked. Thorough cooking (a rolling boil for at least 10 minutes, not a slow cooker alone) destroys the lectin. Green/snap beans eaten raw in small quantities (as a vegetable) are generally safe; the lectin content in immature pods is much lower than in mature dry beans. Dry kidney beans should never be consumed without proper boiling. This is a real toxicity concern for dry bean cookery, not merely a theoretical one
cautionsAndHazards.toxicityAnimals: Raw beans toxic to dogs and cats for the same lectin reasons as humans; cooked plain beans are generally safe in small quantities. Do not leave dry beans where pets can access them in bulk. Deer eat the plants freely — beans are not toxic to deer or other browsers
cautionsAndHazards.allelopathy: None significant; beans are generally regarded as cooperative companions rather than inhibitors

layer.description: Herbaceous layer (annual); pole beans temporarily occupy the low vine/climbing layer during the growing season

roles: Annual Staple Food Crop (primary — one of the most productive annual protein and vegetable crops for the home garden)

Nitrogen Fixer (bacterial symbiosis with Rhizobium leguminosarum; fixes atmospheric nitrogen into plant-available ammonium in root nodules; net positive nitrogen contribution to the soil after the crop's root system is incorporated)
Soil Builder (root residue adds nitrogen and organic matter)
Dynamic companion (Three Sisters system — see Companion Plants)
Succession Crop (rapid turnover allows 2-3 plantings per season in the same bed space)
companionPlants: Corn (traditional Three Sisters companion — corn provides the vertical structure for pole beans to climb while beans fix nitrogen that feeds the heavy corn crop; this is one of the most time-tested and productive polycultures in the history of North American agriculture; interplant pole beans 2 weeks after corn is established so corn leads in height before beans begin to climb). Squash (Three Sisters third element — sprawling squash leaves shade the soil, suppress weeds, and retain moisture for both the corn and beans; any large-leaf summer or winter squash works). Summer savory (traditional bean companion — reputed to deter bean beetle and improve flavor; plant at bed edges). Radishes (may deter bean beetles; rapid germination marks rows). Carrots (compatible root companion; different growth habits avoid competition). Potatoes (traditional companion — beans said to deter Colorado potato beetle; plant at edges of potato rows). Marigolds (Tagetes — nematode suppression; general beneficial insect support)
antagonists: Alliums (onions, garlic, leeks, chives) — traditional reports suggest Allium family members suppress bean growth; avoid interplanting within the bean bed; keep at bed edges or separate beds. Fennel (broadly allelopathic annual — keep all beans away from fennel). Sunflowers (Helianthus annuus — may inhibit bean germination and growth in close proximity; keep separated). Beets (said to inhibit bean growth; traditional companion antagonism, evidence is anecdotal but worth observing)

wildlife.attracts: Bees visit bean flowers for nectar (self-pollination means yield is not dependent on bee visits, but beneficial insects are welcome); Mexican bean beetle parasitoid wasps (Pediobius foveolatus) are attracted to bean plantings where the beetle is present — supporting habitat for these beneficial wasps reduces beetle pressure. Hummingbirds occasionally visit where red-flowered relatives (scarlet runner bean) are grown
wildlife.hostPlantFor: Mexican bean beetle (Epilachna varivestis) — a significant pest that uses Phaseolus as its primary host (see Maintenance); several Epilachna-specialized parasitoid wasps

deer.description: Poor — beans are among the most attractive annual vegetables to deer; the tender foliage, flowers, and pods are heavily browsed; an unprotected bean planting in any area with deer activity will be destroyed before harvest. Physical fencing is the only reliable protection. A standard 7-8' deer fence around the vegetable garden is the permanent solution; electric fence (2-strand) can be effective as a temporary deterrent. The elevated canopy of pole beans on a 5-6' trellis places the pods slightly beyond casual browse reach, but deer will push through and consume pole beans readily if motivated — height alone is not deer protection. Repellent sprays are ineffective on beans because the plants are actively harvested and eaten; you cannot spray deer deterrent on a food crop you intend to eat daily. The vegetable garden must be fenced in any area with regular deer presence

salt.description: Poor — beans are salt-sensitive; do not apply road salt near bean beds; keep away from areas receiving salt spray or runoff. Foliar salt damage appears as marginal leaf burn; root-zone salt causes poor germination and stunted growth. Not suitable for coastal gardens with direct salt spray exposure

drought.description: Poor — beans are not drought-tolerant, particularly during flowering and pod set; drought during bloom causes flower and pod abortion; drought during pod development causes stringy, tough pods and premature seed hardening; consistent moisture is the single most important factor in snap bean quality; mulch heavily (2-3" straw or shredded leaves) to retain moisture and regulate soil temperature

tolerances.other: Cold — beans are genuinely cold-intolerant at all life stages; seeds rot in soil below 60°F (cold soil is the primary cause of germination failure and is especially problematic in heavy clay that stays cold longer than light soil); seedlings are killed by any frost; even chilly nights (below 50°F) delay growth and increase susceptibility to disease; do not rush the season — waiting for consistently warm soil produces faster, healthier plants than early sowing into cold ground. Heat — pod set declines above 90°F and flower drop increases above 95°F; in the Deep South and Desert Southwest, beans perform best as a spring and fall crop rather than a midsummer one; in Zones 8-10, sow early (February-March) and again in late summer (August-September) to avoid peak heat. Humidity — high humidity promotes fungal diseases (anthracnose, rust, white mold); ensure good air circulation between plants and avoid overhead watering in humid climates

maintenance.maintenanceLevel: Low to moderate; beans are not demanding plants but reward consistent harvest attention (especially pole beans) and require monitoring for Mexican bean beetle, which is the primary pest challenge in the eastern US from the Mid-Atlantic through the Midwest
maintenance.pruningNotes: No pruning required for bush beans. For pole beans, pinch the growing tip when vines reach the top of the trellis to redirect energy into pod production rather than continued extension growth. Remove yellowing or diseased leaves promptly. At season end, cut bush bean plants at the soil surface (do not pull — leave roots to decompose and release fixed nitrogen); for pole beans, remove vines from trellis by pulling the entire plant after frost, then cut at the soil surface. Compost healthy spent plants; do not compost plants showing mosaic virus symptoms (destroy or bag for trash).
propagationMethods: Direct sow exclusively — beans do not transplant well due to root sensitivity; starting indoors is not recommended or necessary. Sow seeds 1" deep
4-6" apart
after last frost and when soil is reliably above 60°F. Timing by zone: Zone 3-4 — late May to early June; Zone 5-6 — mid to late May; Zone 7 — early to mid May; Zone 8-9 — March-April for spring crop
August for fall crop; Zone 10 — February-March and September-October. Succession sowing: for continuous bush bean harvest

sow a new row every 2-3 weeks from your safe sowing date through approximately 6-7 weeks before first expected fall frost. Pole beans: one sowing at the start of the season is sufficient — they produce continuously through frost. Inoculate seeds with Rhizobium leguminosarum inoculant before sowing if beans have not been grown in the bed within the past 3-4 years (the bacteria do not persist indefinitely in soil, and a fresh inoculation guarantees active nitrogen fixation; inoculant is inexpensive, widely available, and applied simply by moistening seeds and rolling in the powder before planting). Seed saving: beans are self-pollinating and true to type; allow pods to dry completely on the plant
harvest before rain causes mold
finish drying indoors

store seeds in airtight jars.
Pest & Disease Notes:
Mexican Bean Beetle (Epilachna varivestis): The most significant pest of beans throughout the eastern US
from the Mid-Atlantic through the Midwest and into the Southeast. Adults are copper-colored
oval
and 1/4" long with 16 black spots arranged in 3 rows — they resemble a beneficial ladybug but are distinctly copper-gold (not red) with more spots. Larvae are yellow-orange
soft
and covered with spiny branching projections; they cluster on leaf undersides. Both adults and larvae skeletonize bean leaves by feeding on the lower leaf surface
leaving a lacy

papery upper layer — characteristic damage that makes identification straightforward. Adults overwinter in leaf litter and emerge in late spring to lay bright yellow egg clusters on leaf undersides. MANAGEMENT: Scout the undersides of leaves weekly beginning when plants are established. Hand-pick adults
egg masses

and larvae and drop into soapy water — this is the most effective and least disruptive control method. Removing eggs before they hatch prevents the population explosion that causes serious defoliation. Neem oil (applied to leaf undersides) is effective on larvae. Pyrethrin as a last resort. The parasitoid wasp Pediobius foveolatus is sold commercially and can be released into heavily infested gardens with good effect — it parasitizes larvae and is harmless to plants and humans. Early-season bush beans often escape the worst beetle pressure by maturing before the population peaks in mid-summer. Western US gardeners see less Mexican bean beetle pressure

but other pests (bean fly, aphids, spider mites in arid climates) may be more prominent.
Bean Mosaic Viruses (Bean Common Mosaic Virus, Bean Yellow Mosaic Virus): Seed-borne viruses that cause mottled
puckered

distorted foliage; infected plants are stunted and produce poor yields; there is no cure — affected plants must be removed and destroyed. PREVENTION: Purchase certified disease-free seed from reputable suppliers every year (do not save seed from plants showing any mosaic symptoms); choose resistant varieties (Provider is BCMV-resistant); control aphids (the primary vector of mosaic viruses — aphids feeding on infected plants carry the virus to healthy ones; knock aphids off with a strong water spray or apply insecticidal soap early). Do not compost infected plant material.
Root Rot (Pythium, Fusarium): The primary consequence of cold

wet soil at sowing time. Affected seedlings fail to emerge or collapse at the soil line. Prevention: do not sow until soil is above 60°F; ensure good bed drainage; do not overwater seedlings. In raised beds with amended soil
root rot is rarely a problem.

cultivarNotes:
Phaseolus vulgaris encompasses an extraordinary range of varieties — snap beans, wax beans, purple beans, shell beans, and dry beans — each suited to different harvest goals. The following selections cover the full range of climates and purposes across the lower 48.
**SNAP BEANS — BUSH TYPE** (45-60 days; concentrated harvest; good for canning and freezing; no support needed):
**'Provider'** — the benchmark early bush snap bean; 50 days; round, 5-6" pods; dark green; extremely disease-resistant, including BCMV and powdery mildew; one of the few varieties that will germinate and perform acceptably in cooler soil (55-60°F) — making it the best choice for the first sowing of the season in short-season areas (Zones 3-6); heavy producer; widely available. Also performs well in the humid Southeast and Mid-Atlantic where disease pressure is high.
**'Contender'** — similar to Provider; 48 days; slightly earlier; also good cold-soil performance; heat and humidity tolerant — a strong choice for southern growers (Zones 7-9) where heat tolerance matters as much as cold tolerance. Long history of performance across the eastern US.
**'Blue Lake 274'** — 58 days; the classic standard of snap bean flavor; round, 5-6" pods; excellent fresh eating quality; good for canning; slightly more disease-susceptible than Provider; best planted when soil is fully warm (65°F+), making it ideal for the second or third succession sowing in northern zones or the primary sowing in warmer zones.
**'Jade'** — 60 days; developed for the Pacific Northwest; excellent in cool, maritime climates (Zones 7b-9a along the West Coast); long, straight, dark green pods; productive and disease-resistant; a good alternative to Blue Lake where cool summers and fungal pressure are concerns.
**SNAP BEANS — POLE TYPE** (60-75 days; continuous harvest; higher total yield per plant; superior flavor; needs 5-6' trellis):
**'Blue Lake Pole'** — 60 days to first harvest; the pole version of the Blue Lake flavor standard; round, 5-6" pods; productive and continuous; classic and reliable; excellent fresh eating and canning. Performs well across all zones where the frost-free season exceeds 75 days.
**'Kentucky Wonder'** — 65 days; the American heirloom pole bean standard; flat, slightly curved pods; distinctive robust flavor; string-free when young; tremendously productive; has been grown in North American gardens for over 150 years; excellent dried as a dry bean (large tan seeds). Performs well in all US regions; particularly heat-tolerant.
**'Fortex'** — 60 days; French filet (haricot vert) type; extremely long, slender pods (10-12") of exceptional tenderness and flavor; must be picked young (at pencil-thin stage) for best quality; becomes tough if left on the vine; gourmet quality; the superior fresh-eating pole bean. Requires more frequent harvesting discipline than standard pole beans.
**WAXPOD / YELLOW SNAP BEANS:**
**'Dragon Tongue'** — 57 days; bush type; flat, pale yellow pods with dramatic purple streaks; one of the most beautiful and most versatile beans in the garden; excellent as a snap bean when young; exceptional as a shell bean when pods swell and seeds are fully formed; seeds are cream with burgundy splashes at shell stage; the purple color is a visual ripeness cue (pods are best before they turn fully yellow and dull); very distinctive at a farm stand or in a garden; highly recommended across all zones.
**'Rocdor'** — 55 days; bush wax bean; smooth, round yellow pods; clean, buttery flavor; productive; a workhorse yellow bean. Reliable in Zones 4-9.
**PURPLE SNAP BEANS:**
**'Royal Burgundy'** — 55 days; bush type; round, deep purple pods that turn green when cooked; the purple color makes them easy to spot at harvest among the foliage; good flavor; good germination in cooler soil (like Provider); a fun, practical choice. Excellent for a mixed basket with green and yellow snap beans. Works well in northern zones (3-6) where cool-soil germination is valuable.
**SHELL / SHELLING BEANS** (harvest at the mature-fresh-seed stage, before drying):
**'Cannellini'** — the classic Italian white kidney bean; large, creamy seeds at shell stage; rich, earthy flavor; cook rapidly when fresh-shelled; excellent in soups, salads, and pasta dishes. Grow as a dry bean, harvest early for shell eating, or allow to fully dry for pantry storage.
**'Cranberry / Borlotti'** — 68 days to shell stage; bush type; cream pods with red mottling; seeds at shell stage are cream with burgundy streaks — strikingly beautiful; one of the premier shell beans of European tradition; used in Italian ribollita, pasta e fagioli, and countless bean dishes; the streaking disappears when cooked. 'Vermont Cranberry' is a widely available reliable selection. Performs well in Zones 4-8.
**'Flageolet'** — slender, pale green seeds at shell stage; the classic French shell bean; picked before full maturity for its distinctive pale green color and delicate flavor; a specialty item with a dedicated following; pole or bush types available.
**DRY BEANS** (fully matured and dried; pantry staples; 90-110 days):
**'Black Turtle'** — the standard black bean; 90 days; small, black, glossy seeds; earthy, rich flavor; essential for Latin American cookery; prolific producer; bush type; reliable in Zones 4-9 when sown early enough to allow full maturation before fall frost.
**'Pinto'** — 90 days; the most widely grown dry bean in North America; tan with brown streaks (streaking disappears when cooked); earthy flavor; the foundation of refried beans; bush type; excellent productivity. Best in Zones 5-9 where the frost-free season comfortably exceeds 100 days; marginal in Zone 4 and shorter.
**'Jacob's Cattle'** — 80 days; heirloom New England and Mid-Atlantic dry bean; white with dark red splotches (the pattern resembles a Guernsey cow); excellent flavor; one of the best-tasting dry beans available; holds up well in baking and soups; productive bush type; a regional heirloom with deep roots in northeastern US foodways. The shorter maturity (80 days) makes it a strong choice for Zones 4-5 where longer dry beans are risky.
**'Hidatsa Red'** — 85 days; deep red seeds; rich, full flavor; heritage variety from the Hidatsa nation of the upper Missouri River; excellent in chili and soups; high protein content; an outstanding dry bean that deserves wider cultivation; bush type; good drought tolerance for a bean — a useful trait in the Great Plains and intermountain West where summer rain is less reliable.
**'Mortgage Lifter'** (dry bean, not the tomato) — if available; large, pinkish-tan seeds; good flavor; heirloom.
**REGIONAL NOTES:**
**Zones 3-5 (northern tier, short season):** Prioritize early-maturing varieties — Provider and Contender for snap beans; Royal Burgundy for cool-soil germination; Jacob's Cattle and Hidatsa Red for dry beans (80-85 days). Dry bean production is feasible but requires sowing at the earliest safe date. Pole beans need at least 90 frost-free days to justify the trellis investment.
**Zones 6-7 (mid-latitude, moderate season):** The full range of varieties performs well. Succession planting of bush beans is highly productive. Dry beans of all maturities (90-110 days) are reliable. Mexican bean beetle is the primary pest concern in the eastern portion of this band.
**Zones 8-10 (southern tier, long/hot season):** Heat-induced flower drop is the primary challenge — plant early (February-April depending on zone) and again in late summer (August-September) to bracket the hottest months. Contender and Kentucky Wonder are particularly heat-tolerant. Avoid midsummer sowings in Zone 9-10 unless shade cloth is used. Rust and bacterial blight are more persistent in humid southern climates; choose resistant varieties.

suggestedUse: Primary annual vegetable crop; kitchen garden staple; raised beds (preferred); in-ground with amendment; Three Sisters polyculture; succession-planted for continuous harvest; dry bean production for long-term food storage
gardenUse.designPlacement: Beans belong in the vegetable garden as a core annual rotation crop, not in perennial herb sections or ornamental beds. Place bush beans in dedicated beds or rows with full sun exposure and no overhead shade from taller crops or structures. Pole beans require a 5-6' trellis, teepee, or fence — orient the trellis structure on the north side of the vegetable garden so it does not cast shade on lower crops to the south (reverse this in southern hemisphere gardens). The trellis can double as support for cucumbers or other climbers in rotation years. Dry beans can be grown in broader in-ground rows or blocks since they need more space than raised bed real estate typically offers. Three Sisters planting: if growing corn, run a pole bean ring around the base of each corn hill — a historically validated, genuinely productive combination. Beans should rotate through the vegetable garden annually; do not grow beans in the same bed space year after year (build-up of soil pathogens, particularly Fusarium); a 3-year rotation (beans, then brassicas or leafy greens, then roots, then beans) maintains soil health

pennsylvaniaHegins.nativeToSite: No — not native to Pennsylvania or to North America north of Mexico; no naturalization concern; annual, does not persist
pennsylvaniaHegins.paInvasiveStatus: Not regulated in Pennsylvania; not on the PA noxious weed list or DCNR invasive species list. Same as general Invasive field — no concern
pennsylvaniaHegins.drainage: The red clay at Hegins is compacted and drainage-impaired — in-ground bean production in unamended clay is not recommended. Raised beds are strongly preferred and provide the drainage, soil warmth, and aeration that beans require. If growing dry beans in-ground (they need more space than raised beds typically offer), amend with 3-4" compost and mound rows 6" above grade. The raised beds on site warm faster than the surrounding clay ground, which is a meaningful advantage for a crop that requires 60°F soil to germinate
pennsylvaniaHegins.soil: The Hegins red clay requires amendment before bean planting — work 3-4" of compost into the top 8-10" for in-ground beds, or use the existing raised beds which already provide suitable texture. Do not add nitrogen fertilizer; the clay's natural fertility plus compost is sufficient, and added N suppresses the Rhizobium symbiosis that makes beans valuable in the rotation
pennsylvaniaHegins.cultivarNotes: Grow Provider as the snap bean backbone (earliest, most disease-resistant, most forgiving of cool clay soil); add Fortex or Blue Lake Pole on a dedicated 6' trellis for continuous summer harvest; grow Dragon Tongue for variety, shell-bean eating, and beauty; grow Jacob's Cattle or Black Turtle as a dry bean for the pantry. This combination covers all three harvest stages. Sow Provider first (around May 15-20 when clay soil reaches 60°F), then succession-sow bush beans every 2-3 weeks through mid-July. Pole beans: one sowing in late May produces through October frost.
pennsylvaniaHegins.designPlacement: Beans are destined for the raised vegetable beds within a fenced area — they cannot be grown in Sections A, B, or C (ornamental and herb beds) and must not be placed in unfenced areas due to deer. Within the fenced vegetable area: bush beans (Provider, Dragon Tongue, Royal Burgundy) in the raised beds in 2-3 week succession from late May through mid-July — they are the workhorse of the summer vegetable harvest and a strong choice for the raised bed space because they turn over quickly, leave nitrogen in the soil, and can be followed by a fall crop. Pole beans (Fortex, Blue Lake Pole) on a dedicated 5-6' trellis along the north side of the vegetable bed, where their height shades nothing of consequence; the trellis can be a permanent bamboo, wood, or metal structure reused each year. Dry beans (Jacob's Cattle, Black Turtle) can be grown in a dedicated in-ground row or block within the fenced area — they do not need raised bed real estate and will tolerate moderately amended clay
pennsylvaniaHegins.siteObservations: The deer pressure at Hegins is the single largest obstacle to bean production — without a fence, the crop will not survive to harvest. This must be the first infrastructure investment before any significant vegetable growing is attempted. Once fenced, beans are among the most rewarding and productive annual vegetables for this site. The raised beds provide the drainage and soil warmth advantage that beans reward. Soil temperature in the clay-heavy ground at Hegins is the second key variable — do not sow before the soil has reached 60°F consistently, which typically means waiting until May 20-25 rather than the traditional "after last frost" date of approximately May 1. The raised beds warm faster than in-ground soil and permit slightly earlier sowing. Mexican bean beetle should be anticipated as a recurring pest; plan to scout leaf undersides weekly from June onward and hand-pick aggressively. Rhizobium inoculant should be purchased and applied to seeds at first sowing, and re-applied any year beans are sown in a bed that has not grown legumes in the previous 2-3 years. The nitrogen-fixing benefit of beans is substantial over years of garden rotation and is one of the strongest arguments for making them a permanent feature of the annual vegetable rotation. Salt is not a concern for the vegetable area — the beds are well away from the salted road on the north border.
